coredata.dc:title: All-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction for highly efficient degradation of ofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Chunsheng
Guo, Jun
Chen, Peng
Gan, Wei
Yin, Zhuangzhuang
Qi, Shihan
Zhang, Miao
Sun, Zhaoqi
coredata.dc:description: An all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/TiO2 heterojunction was successfully prepared using a two-step hydrothermal method. Under simulated sunlight, samples exhibited the best photoelectrochemical properties, resulting in enhanced degradation of ofloxacin. The 0.2In2S3/CQDs-2/titanium dioxide nanoarray (TNR) exhibited the highest photocurrent density, which was 16.9 times higher than that of the original TNR. The visible-light degradation rate of ofloxacin was 92.7% after 120 min, and the first-order kinetic constant was 0.0186 min−1 (7.41 and 7.01 times higher than those of the original TNR and In2S3, respectively). The excellent photoelectrochemical performance was mainly attributed to the CQDs acting as an electron mediator, promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and maintaining high redox capacity. A mechanism of an all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction for the degradation of ofloxacin was proposed, providing a novel approach for the degradation of ofloxacin by semiconductor photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: All-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction for highly efficient degradation of ofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, Anhui 230601, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/TiO2 heterojunction was successfully prepared using a two-step hydrothermal method. Under simulated sunlight, samples exhibited the best photoelectrochemical properties, resulting in enhanced degradation of ofloxacin. The 0.2In2S3/CQDs-2/titanium dioxide nanoarray (TNR) exhibited the highest photocurrent density, which was 16.9 times higher than that of the original TNR. The visible-light degradation rate of ofloxacin was 92.7% after 120 min, and the first-order kinetic constant was 0.0186 min−1 (7.41 and 7.01 times higher than those of the original TNR and In2S3, respectively). The excellent photoelectrochemical performance was mainly attributed to the CQDs acting as an electron mediator, promoting the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and maintaining high redox capacity. A mechanism of an all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction for the degradation of ofloxacin was proposed, providing a novel approach for the degradation of ofloxacin by semiconductor photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been extensive research on antibiotics since the discovery of penicillin, which was undoubtedly a technological revolution [1]. Antibiotics are now widely used around the world to cure diseases [2,3]. However, some antibiotics are not completely metabolized by the human body, and eventually make their way into the environment. constituting one of the major causes of water resource contamination by medicines [4]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), antibiotics are present in surface water and groundwater in many countries. The misuse of antibiotics is on the rise, and such prevalence means that they are concentrated in the human body following the consumption of water. This circulation of antibiotics has been classified as a threat to human health [5,6].

In recent years, as a representative third-generation fluoroquinolone (FQ) antibiotic, ofloxacin (OFL) has been widely used in the clinical treatment of infectious diseases [7,8]. However, it has been reported that low concentrations of ofloxacin can accumulate in water environments for prolonged periods while resisting degradation, which in turn allows bacteria to develop resistance to antibiotics through selection and induction processes, potentially reducing the ability of the human immune system to fight off pathogens [9,10]. Therefore, the development of efficient treatment methods is critical from the perspective of health and economics.

Physical adsorption [11,12], chemical degradation [13] and biodegradation techniques [14] have been used extensively for the treatment of ofloxacin in water. However, owing to the chemical stability of antiobiotics, the aforementioned techniques do not completely degrade them. Owing to their economic, stable, and efficient characteristics, photocatalytic degradation techniques have been applied in recent years for the degradation of organic pollutants [15], reduction of carbon dioxide [16], reduction of chromium [17] and antibiotic decomposition [18]. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has attracted considerable attention in the field of photocatalysis because of its low cost, excellent stability, and non-toxicity [19–21]. However, TiO2 has a wide band gap (3.0–3.2 eV) which is underutilized for visible light. Moreover, a rapid electron-hole complexation rate is generated by photoexcitation. Owing to these limitations, further potential applications of TiO2 are restricted. Therefore, effective strategies should be adopted for solving such problems. Coupling TiO2 with narrow-bandgap semiconductors to construct heterojunctions is an effective strategy to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [22,23], which not only enhances the absorption of visible light, but also reduces the electron-hole complexation rate.

Indium sulfide (In2S3), a narrow bandgap (2.0–2.3 eV) semiconductor, has been extensively explored in photocatalysis and most commonly used to form composite heterojunctions with titanium dioxide [24,25]. However, due to the larger work function and lower Fermi energy level of TiO2, and the lower work function and higher Fermi energy level of In2S3, electrons are easily transferred from the conduction band (CB) of In2S3 to the CB of TiO2 under the visible-light excitation. According to previous reports on In2S3-TiO2 composites, such as photocatalytic hydrogen production [26–28], photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [25,29–32], and solar cells [33–35], it is apparent that the mechanism most commonly comprises a conventional type-II charge transfer mechanism or a unidirectional transfer of electrons from the CB of In2S3 to the CB of TiO2, and it is ascribed to the two semiconductor energy bands and Fermi energy levels. A composite of these two semiconductors can effectively increase the carrier transfer efficiency; however, it may compromise the redox ability of the composite. The reduction ability of electrons in the CB of In2S3 and the oxidation ability of holes in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 according to electron transfer in the type-II heterojunction are shown in Scheme 1 (a). The type-II heterojunction is switched to an all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction by introducing an electron mediator and changing the electron transfer path. This retains carriers with strong redox abilities in bands with strong redox potentials. In all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunctions, noble metals (such as Ag, Au, and Pt) [36,37] are mainly available as electronic mediators, while Guo et al. reported a ternary nanocomposite photocatalyst with Ag as an electron transfer bridge, exhibiting efficient degradation performance for organic pollutants under visible light [38]. Ji et al. demonstrated that the higher affinity for sulfur atoms makes Au the most suitable choice for coupling with In2S3. The constructed ternary Z-scheme In2S3@Au@P3HT photocatalyst has the dual function of phenolic degradation and Cr(VI) reduction [39]. Wang et al. combined graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and three-dimensionally ordered macroporous carbon-encapsulated TiO2 (3DOM-TiO2@C) with Pt nanoparticles as an electron transfer system to selectively boost the visible-light-driven conversion of CO2 to CH4 [40]. However, noble metals are not abundant in nature, and their extraction cost is high, which are the major barriers to preparing low-cost photocatalysts. Compared with noble metal nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [41], graphene quantum dots [42], and Ti3C2 quantum dots [43] have high dispersion, good electrical conductivity, and large surface area, and thus, these can potentially replace noble metal nanoparticles as electronic mediators (Scheme 1(b)).

In this study, we designed and successfully fabricated an all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction with CQDs as an electron mediator, characterized the chemical composition, structure, and photoelectrochemical properties of the samples using several characterization techniques, and experimentally determined the optimal loading amount of CQDs. Compared with previous reports, it was found that the prepared samples had highly efficient visible photocatalytic activity for ofloxacin through catalytic degradation experiments, allowing us to propose a photocatalytic degradation mechanism.
All reagents used in this experiment were analytical-grade and used without further purification.
In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction was prepared using a two-step hydrothermal method.

Step 1: A titanium dioxide nanoarray (TNR) was prepared on FTO conductive glass (which was cut into 2 × 5 cm2 pieces), mixed with hydrochloric acid, deionized water, and tetrabutyl titanate in a volume ratio of 30:30:1, and stirred vigorously until the mixed solution was homogeneous. Finally, the FTO conductive glass was placed in an autoclave with the conductive side down on a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor lining, and the solution was slowly poured into it; the temperature and time of the oven were set to 150 °C and 720 min, respectively. After the reaction, the samples were rinsed three times with alcohol and deionized water and placed in an oven at 60 °C for drying.

Step 2: The In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction was prepared again using the hydrothermal method, where polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was the source of the CQDs [44], and the content of the CQDs was controlled by adjusting the amount of PVP. Briefly, 0.2 mmol of InCl3 was mixed with 1 mmol of L-cysteine in 30 mL deionized water and stirred for 30 min. PVP was added to the above solution and stirred continuously for 30 min; thereafter, the prepared TNR was applied to the PTFE reactor lining. Finally, the solution was poured into it, and the oven was set at 180 °C for 720 min. The subsequent treatments were the same as those for the TNR. The amount of PVP added was 0.1, 0.2 and 0.3 mg, and labeled as 0.2In/CQDs-1/TNR, 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR and 0.2In/CQDs-3/TNR, respectively. For comparison, 0.1In/TNR, 0.2In/TNR, and 0.3In/TNR without CQDs were prepared, corresponding to 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 mmol of InCl3, respectively. In addition, we prepared the In2S3 sample on FTO using the same method as follows: 0.2 mmol InCl3 and 1 mmol of L-cysteine were mixed in 30 mL of deionized water and stirred for 30 min, followed by placing FTO in a volume of 50 mL of Teflon liner (conductive side down) and subsequently transferred to an autoclave for 12 h at 180 °C. The subsequent treatments were the same as those used for the preparation of In2S3/CQDs/TiO2. This process is illustrated in Scheme 2 .

Using Cu Kα as the source of radiation, an X-ray diffractometer (MAC, M18XHF) was capable of detecting the crystal structure and phase information of the samples. The elemental composition and surface morphology were measured using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Hitachi-regulus 8230). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM-2100) was used for lattice spacing measurements. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB-250) was used to analyze the elemental composition of the sample surface. Thermal stability of sample was analyzed with thermogravimetric analyser (TGA5500). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of the samples were acquired using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FL, Hitachi F-4500) and an ultrafast time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Flouro-Max plus). Light absorption performance was analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2550). The electron paramagnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker EMX Plus) was used for electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis. Total Organic Carbon Analyzer (TOC-L CPH, Shimadzu, Japan) was used to total organic carbon (TOC) analysis.

Photoelectrochemical properties of the samples were tested on a three-electrode electrochemical workstation (CHI660D). Platinum was used as the working electrode, and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Using 0.1 M Na2SO4 solution as the electrolyte, the transient photocurrent test was performed by cutting the sample into a 1 × 2 cm2 size, pointing the film side to the platinum electrode, and turning the light on/off every 20 s for 5 cycles. The AC impedance test and Mott–Schottky and linear scan voltammetry measurements were performed in the absence of light. The sample size was the same as that used for the photocurrent test.
All photocatalytic tests were performed at room temperature.

Before the test, deionized water was poured into a quartz cuvette (10 mL), and the baseline was calibrated using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Subsequently, the configured concentration of 25 mg/L ofloxacin (the solvent was deionized water) was injected into a quartz cuvette (volume = 10 mL) and tested in the wavelength range of 200–420 nm with a UV–visible spectrophotometer. The maximum absorption wavelength of ofloxacin was obtained at approximately 290 nm. Samples with a size of 1 × 1 cm2 size were placed in special containers for photocatalytic degradation (placed with the film side facing upward) and subsequently placed in dark conditions for 40 min to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium.
The specific calculation method is described below.

We performed similar photocatalytic experiments under different pH and inorganic anion conditions, in which the pH of ofloxacin was adjusted by 1 M HCl and 1 M NaOH, so that the final pH of the 25 mg/L ofloxacin solution was 4.8. The inorganic anions were provided by positive salts containing Na+ ions (NaCO3, NaCl, and Na2SO4) at a content of 2 mmol.

To investigate the active substances that play a major role in the catalytic process, benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO), and isopropanol (IPA) were added to the OFL solution with the best photocatalytic performance for capturing superoxide radicals (
O2 −), holes (h+), and hydroxyl radicals(
OH).

XRD patterns of the samples are presented in Fig. 1 . Comparing the diffraction peaks of the other samples with those of the FTO conductive glass, (101), (111), (002), (301), and (112) crystal planes of TiO2 appear at 36.08°, 41.22°, 62.74°, 69.00°, and 69.78°, respectively, which are typical of the rutile phase (JCPDS No. 21-1276) (Fig. 1(c)). The characteristic peaks at 27.4° and 47.7° correspond to the (311) and (440) crystal planes of In2S3, respectively (JCPDS No. 32-0456) (Fig. 1(e)). In addition, Fig. 1(c) and (d) show the diffraction peaks of all substances mentioned previously. Unfortunately, the diffraction peaks of the CQDs are not found in Fig. 1(d), which may be attributed to the small volume and low content of CQDs.

Fig. 2 (a) illustrates the surface morphology of the original TNR, and it can be seen that titanium dioxide nanorods, with an average diameter of approximately 150 nm, were grown vertically on the FTO substrate. The In2S3 nanosheets were staggered and stacked into a mesh-like structure, and the thickness of the nanosheets was thin (approximately 30 nm) (Fig. 2(b)). The morphologies of the TNR and In2S3 composites are shown in Fig. 2(c), which is different from that shown in Fig. 2(b). In2S3 was grown on the TNR in a fragmented pattern, providing a higher specific surface area and more active sites to a certain degree, and it was favorable for the catalytic reaction. The morphology of the In2S3/TNR with the CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(d)–(f). When the CQDs content was increased, In2S3 changes from nanofragments to saturated nanoflowers, further increasing the number of active sites. The EDS energy spectrum analysis (Fig. 2(g)), it was found that elements such as Ti, O, In, S, and C were present in the 0.2 In/CQDs-1/TNR sample, confirming the successful synthesis of the sample.

Fig. 3 (a) presents the TEM images of the 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR. The dimensions of the TNR better resolved, and some nanoflakes attached to the TNR were evident. The HR-TEM images of 0.2 In/CQDs-2/TNR are shown in Fig. 3(b)-(d). The lattice distances of 0.249 and 0.324 nm correspond to the (101) and (110) crystal planes of the rutile-phase TNR, respectively. Moreover, in Fig. 3(c), nanosheets attached to the TNR with a lattice spacing of 0.268 nm were also observed, corresponding to the (400) crystal plane of In2S3. Nanoparticles with a diameter of approximately 5 nm were attached to the TNR with a lattice spacing of 0.321 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(d), which is consistent with the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [45].

The surface chemical states and elemental compositions of the samples were analyzed using XPS, and XPS profiles of the TNR, In2S3, 0.2In/TNR, and 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR are shown in Fig. 4 (a). 0.2In/TNR and 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR also contained elements from the TNR and In2S3. Fig. 4(b) and (d) show the Ti 2p and In 3d peaks, respectively. For the pure TNR sample and the pure In2S3 sample: the binding energies located at 458.6 and 464.4 eV correspond to the characteristic peaks at Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, indicating the presence of Ti4+, and the binding energies at 444.8 and 452.3 eV correspond to the peaks at In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2, respectively [46,47]. In the 0.2In/TNR sample, the binding energies of Ti 2p and In 3d exhibited a slight change compared to the single TNR and In2S3, suggesting a compact interaction and strong chemical interactions at the interface of TNR and In2S3. In general, the binding energy of Ti 2p in the 0.2In/TNR sample shifted toward the low binding energy region compared to TNR, while the In 3d of 0.2In/TNR shifted toward a higher binding energy compared to pure In2S3. This is attributed to the fact that the Fermi energy level of In2S3 is higher than that of TNR [24]. When In2S3 is combined with TNR, the electrons on In2S3 are transferred to TNR to obtain the Fermi energy level equilibrium. The positive charge due to lost electrons is retained in In2S3, while the electron density on TNR increases, so that the interface between In2S3 and TNR is charged; thus generating a weak internal electric field at the interface. In summary, during the XPS characterization, both In2S3 and TNR are photoexcited, and under the influence of the internal electric field, photogenerated electrons migrate from In2S3 to TNR. This migration mechanism of photogenerated electrons is consistent with type-II heterojunctions [48,49]. After the introduction of CQDs, the peaks in 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR shifted in the opposite direction, compared with those of Ti 2p and In 3d in 0.2In/TNR. The binding energy of Ti 2p increased, indicating that TNR lost electrons, while the binding energy of In 3d decreased, indicating that In2S3 gained electrons. The change in binding energy may be due to the possible role of CQDs as an electron mediator altering the electron transport path [50,51]. The O 1s peaks in Fig. 4(c) could be deconvoluted into two independent peaks: the lattice oxygen (Ti-O) of the TNR and surface hydroxyl oxygen (OH). The binding of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 is shown in Fig. 4(e) with a difference of 1.2 eV, confirming the presence of S2−. The C 1s XPS of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.3 eV correspond to the CC, CO, and COC groups, respectively, thus proving the successful introduction of CQDs [52] (Fig. 4(f)). CQDs as an electron mediator promoted the interfacial charge transfer between the TNR and In2S3, demonstrating the emergence of an all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction that enhances the photocatalytic performance.

Optical properties of the samples are shown in Fig. 5 . Owing to its wide bandgap, TNR only absorbs visible light below 400 nm, whereas In2S3 absorbs visible light at approximately 600 nm, with a stronger absorption due to the inherent bandgap absorption [53]. Therefore, when the TNR is compounded with In2S3, the light absorption ability is superior to that of the original TNR. When loaded with CQDs, the light absorption intensity was further enhanced, and the color of the sample changed from brownish red to yellowish, as shown in Fig. S1, providing further evidence for the successful loading of the CQDs. Meanwhile, using the Tauc formula, the bandgap widths of TNR and In2S3 can be obtained as follows:

As shown in Fig. 5(b), the value of n is 1 for direct-transition semiconductors, and 4 for indirect semiconductors. n is taken as 1 for both TNR and In2S3, which are direct-transition semiconductors. Based on the data, it was estimated that the band gaps of TNR and In2S3 were 3.01 and 2.17 eV, respectively. The valence band potentials (EVB) of the TNR and In2S3 were obtained from the VB-XPS spectra (Fig. 5(c)), with values of 2.75 eV and 1.05 eV, respectively. Due to the contact potential difference between the sample and the XPS analyzer, the valence band potential of the samples at the standard hydrogen electrode can be calculated according to the following equation [54]:

The corresponding CB potentials (ECB) of TNR and In2S3 were −0.15 and − 1.01 eV. The energy band structures of TNR and In2S3 were plotted by combining these results, as shown in Fig. 5(d).

Photoluminescence spectroscopy and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy were used to investigate the complex efficiency of photogenerated electrons, charge mobility, and carrier decay lifetime of the semiconductors. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the higher the PL peak, the higher the compounding efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs, and the lower the charge mobility [55]. The highest peak of the original TNR shows a significant decrease in photoluminescence intensity when In2S3 and CQDs were introduced, which may be due to the favorable reduction of the electron-hole pair compounding by the charge transfer mechanism of the all-solid-state Z-scheme. Finally, sample 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR had the lowest luminescence intensity compared to the other samples, and therefore the best optical properties. Fig. 6(b) shows the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of the samples (the excitiation wavelength was 325 nm). 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR has a significantly longer average lifetime (12.4 ps) than TNR (2.2 ps) and 0.2In/TNR (5.4 ps), indicating that the loading of CQDs serves as an electron mediator, effectively prolonging the carrier lifetime and reducing the compounding rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the addition of CQDs as electron mediators widens the face-to-face contact interface, forming massive high-speed charge transfer channels [56]. The formation of more catalytic interfaces supports prolonged carrier lifetime and effective carrier separation, which is greatly beneficial for photocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of the prepared material
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the thermal stability of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR, thermogravimetric analysis was performed and the result was shown in Fig. S2. Slight weight loss at up to 200 °C, which could be attributed to the evaporation of small molecules from the sample powder and the residual surface further release of organic matters from the surface, with no loss of CQDs, indicating that the CQDs were relatively stable up to 200 °C, and between 200 and 400 °C, the weight loss could be attributed to the evaporation of CQDs and species that were anchored to the CQDs[57]. The significant weight loss temperature was located at 450–550 °C, corresponding to the oxidation of In2S3, and the weight loss process stopped at around 550 °C, indicating the complete conversion of In2S3 to In2O3 in the sample 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR[58]. Due to the fact that the TNR fabricated in this paper is the most stable rutile phase of all the crystalline phases of TiO2, there is no transition in the crystalline phase at 550-700°C, thus, no weight loss occurs. Thermogravimetric analysis showed that, barring unavoidable circumstances, the samples we prepared had relatively excellent stability.

Electrical properties of the samples were investigated using an electrochemical workstation, as shown in Fig. 7 . It was observed that the transient photocurrent density of the TNR was able to achieve 10 μA/cm2 under visible-light illumination, and with the introduction of In2S3 the sample 0.2In/TNR had the best photocurrent density, approximately three times higher than that of the original TNR (Fig. 7(a) and (b)). This improved further when the CQDs were introduced into the In2S3/TNR system. 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR exhibited the largest photocurrent density, reaching as far as 169 μA/cm2, which was 5.3 and 16.9 times of 0.2In/TNR and TNR, respectively. With the addition of CQDs content, the photocurrent density decreased, which might be due to the aggregation of excessive CQDs and covering of the original active sites. This increases the number of novel photogenerated electron-hole complex centers. The interfacial charge transfer of the samples was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy, and a Nyquist plot was drawn (Fig. 7(c)). 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR had the minimum radius of curvature, indicating that its interfacial transfer resistance was the minimum, and the interfacial charge transfer rate was the highest. Linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was used to characterize the reducibility of the samples, as shown in Fig. 7(d). The 0.2 In/CQDs-2/TNR sample had the highest current density at the same voltage, meaning that its overpotential was the lowest. This would suggest that CQDs are beneficial for the improvement of the catalytic reduction activity of the In2S3/TNR system. The flat-band potentials of the pure TNR and In2S3 samples were also measured (Fig. S3), and the positive slope indicates that both TNR and In2S3 are n-type semiconductors. Combined with the above results, we propose that CQDs, as electron mediators, promote the separation of electron-hole pairs of In2S3 and TNR, while simultaneously inhibiting the complexation of electrons and holes.

The samples were placed in the OFL solution under dark conditions and allowed to reach adsorption equilibrium (40 min) prior to the photocatalytic degradation process. The samples were subsequently measured under simulated sunlight. Photocatalytic degradation efficiency was calculated using the following empirical equation:

× 100 %
where C0 and Ct are the concentrations after adsorption of the solution and illumination for t min, respectively. A0 is the absorbance after adsorption equilibrium, and At is the absorbance after illumination for t min. The photocatalytic degradation efficiencies of all the samples are shown in Fig. 8 (a) and (b). The results show that the degradation efficiency of the photocatalysts with CQDs loading was significantly higher than that of the other samples. 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR had the optimal degradation rate for OFL after 120 min (92.7%), which was 3.63 and 3.46 times of the original TNR (25.5%) and In2S3 (26.8%), respectively. This is explained by the fact that the CQDs act as electronic mediators to enhance charge transfer between the TNR and In2S3. The degradation curve of 0.2 In/CQDs-2/TNR is shown in Fig. S4. To investigate the adsorption capacity of the samples, experiments were performed in the dark for up to 30 min (Fig. S5). The results show that a certain loading of CQDs enhanced the adsorption of the composite structure of TNR and In2S3, which is attributed to the unique geometry of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR, resulting in a larger specific surface area, increasing the adsorption probability of OFL molecules.

To quantitatively analyze the catalytic process of OFL, the kinetic curves of all catalysts are presented in Fig. 8(c) and (d). The kinetic curves of all samples correspond to the first-order model,
(5)
ln
C 0
C t
= K t
where t is the illumination time, K is the kinetic rate constant, and C0 and Ct are the concentrations after adsorption of the solution and after illumination for t min, respectively. The kinetic constants of all the samples are more directly represented in Fig. 8(e) and (f). Among them, the kinetic constants of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR were 7.41 and 3.46 times higher than those of TNR and 0.2In/TNR, respectively. However, as the content of CQDs increases, the value of K first increases and then decreases, which may be due to excess CQDs leading to agglomeration between quantum dots, and a decreased contact area between the CQDs and the two semiconductors, reducing the electron transmission ability.

Photocatalysts require excellent efficiency and stability. After five cycles of continuous degradation, the experiments were performed on the final sample (Fig. 9 (a)), and the degradation efficiency of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR for OFL did not decrease significantly, suggesting that 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR has favorable stability. Moreover, the XRD diffraction pattern of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR was observed after five cycles, and the diffraction peaks were not significantly shifted or reduced (Fig. S6). The above results suggest that 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR has good stability for the photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin. Radical-trapping experiments were performed to explore the active species in the photocatalytic reaction process (Fig. 9(b)). After testing the final degradation efficiency after 120 min of illumination, it was found that the degradation efficiency of the OFL sample was the lowest with the addition of BQ (
O2 − scavenger), indicating that
O2 − was the major active species for the photocatalytic degradation of OFL, consistent with previous reports [59]. AO (h+ scavenger) and IPA (
OH scavenger) also inhibited catalytic degradation to a certain degree, and the final results show that the degradation of OFL is the result of the interaction of various active species.

Naturally polluted water bodies may possess different pH values and multiple inorganic anions. Considering the general applicability of the catalysts, we investigated the degradation of OFL by 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR under different pH and inorganic anion conditions. The results are shown in Fig. 9(c) and (d). We discovered that 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR has excellent degradation efficiency for OFL in a wide pH range (5–9), which is representative of polluted water conditions. However, there is a significant decrease in photocatalytic performance under too low or too high pH. This is due to the decrease in active species at lower and higher pH. When pH is strongly acidic,
OH reacts with H+ (
OH + H++e−=H2O) [60], whereas, under alkaline conditions, OH− combines with h+ (4OH−+4h+=O2 + H2O) [61]. Under inorganic anion conditions, CO3 2− has the greatest effect on the catalytic performance of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR because CO3 2− is capable of trapping reactive radicals, such as
O2 − [62]. SO4 2− and Cl− can adsorb on the catalyst surface, and the shielding effect on the active sites is insufficient [63]. Therefore, SO4 2− and Cl− only slightly influenced the catalytic performance of 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR.

The degradation pathways of OFL are generally divided into two types: the cleavage of the piperazine ring and the substitution of the fluorine atom [64,65]. Accordingly, we combined the relevant reports to provide the three most probable degradation paths, as shown in Fig. 10 . In pathway one, h+ and OH− interact to enable OFL to remove the methyl group and form TP1 (m/z, 348), with subsequent reactions of piperazine ring cleavage and decarboxylation, forming organics with m/z values of 320, 279, and 235 (TP2, TP3, TP4), respectively [66,67]. In pathway two, h+ broke the linkage bond between the piperazine ring and benzene ring to form TP5 (m/z, 280) and TP6 (m/z, 101). Subsequently, TP6 underwent cleavage of the piperazine ring to eventually form TP9 (m/z, 87), while TP5 under the influence of
O2 − formed TP7 (m/z, 318) [68]. In pathway three, fluorine was substituted because of the effect of OH (TP10), and the piperazine ring was dealkylated to form TP11 (m/z = 346). In addition, under the mineralization results, pathway three is also a high yield of one of the key pathways for fluorine [2]. The total organic carbon (TOC) and total carbon (TC) tests were further applied to evaluate the mineralization ability of the photocatalyst on OFL, and the results are shown in Fig. S7. The values of TOC/TC gradually decreased as the reaction time proceeded, indicating that OFL was gradually degraded and successfully mineralized during the reaction, and the final TOC removal rate could reach 31.25%, which was consistent with the results of our analysis of degradation paths.

EPR patterns using 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap for EPR analysis are shown in Fig. 11 . The dark conditions did not show any signal of
O2 −, whereas in light conditions, the signal of
O2 − produced in 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR was significantly stronger than that of the single material In2S3 (Fig. 11(a)). Similarly, no signal of
OH was found in the absence of light, and a weak signal under light illumination, while 0.2In/CQDs-2/TNR produced a more obvious signal of
OH compared to TNR (Fig. 11(b)). The EPR results further demonstrate that the all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction has a stronger redox capacity than that of the single component.

Based on XPS, trapping experiments, and EPR analysis, we propose a reaction mechanism for the all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction photocatalytic degradation of OFL, as shown in Fig. 12 . Under visible-light irradiation, photogenerated electron-hole pairs are generated in the valence bands of TNR and In2S3, and the electrons are excited from the valence band of the semiconductors to the CB, as shown in Fig. 5 (d). The CBs of TNR and In2S3 are located at −0.15 and −1.01 eV, respectively, and the required potential for the reduction of O2 to
O2 − is −0.33 eV. It is obvious that the reduction reaction must be completed in the CB of In2S3 in order to produce
O2 −. Similarly, the valence band potentials of TNR and In2S3 are located at 2.86 and 1.16 eV, respectively, and the reduction potentials of
OH are located at 1.98 and 2.67 eV. Therefore the oxidation reaction would have to be completed in the TNR valence band, and the conventional type-II In2S3/TNR cannot accomplish this. Thus, CQDs were introduced as the electron mediator between In2S3 and TNR. Subsequently, the low-reducing electrons on the TNR CB, combined with the low-oxidizing holes on the In2S3 valence band through the CQDs, enabled the retention of the highly oxidizing holes on the TNR valence band and the highly reducing electrons on the In2S3 conduction band, producing large amounts of
OH and
O2 −. The all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction is capable of fully exploiting the redox ability of both semiconductors, thus driving the photocatalytic redox OFL process. The specific reaction process for the photocatalytic degradation of OFL is as follows.

Comparing the results of this study with other studies on the degradation of ofloxacin (Table S1), the other works are generally powder-type photocatalysts, which, in conclusion, may lead to secondary contamination of the water body due to sample recovery difficulties. The thin-film photocatalysts are able to overcome this deficiency, and at the same time, ensure excellent performance of the catalyst.

A novel all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction was successfully fabricated for the photocatalytic degradation of ofloxacin by growing In2S3 nanosheets on one-dimensional TiO2 nanorods with the introduction of CQDs. Several characterization methods were used to characterize it, and the results confirmed that the all-solid-state Z-scheme In2S3/CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction has optimal photoelectrochemical properties, which can enhance the charge transfer and separation effectively and maintain strong redox ability and stability. With these advantages, 0.2In2S3/CQDs-2/TNR showed a high degradation of 92.7% of OFL in 120 min, which was a significant improvement compared to the original TNR and In2S3. This work not only provides a new method for the preparation of novel all-solid Z-scheme heterojunctions, but also offers an economical, efficient, and sustainable method for the degradation of ofloxacin.
